channel.title: Wasil Zafar
channel.description: Technical articles on embedded systems, AI, software architecture, business strategy, philosophy, and life sciences.

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 12: Evolutionary Genomics
item.description: Genomics revolution in evolutionary biology — comparative genomics, gene duplication & genome evolution, horizontal gene transfer, transposable elements, epigenetics & inheritance, CRISPR in evolutionary research, metagenomics, phylogenomics, and future frontiers.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 11: Paleontology & Fossil Interpretation
item.description: Reading the fossil record — radiometric dating techniques, index fossils, transitional fossils (Tiktaalik, Archaeopteryx), taphonomy, trace fossils, fossilization processes, biostratigraphy, and integrating paleontological data with molecular evidence.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 10: Mathematical & Theoretical Evolution
item.description: Mathematical models in evolutionary biology — fitness landscapes, adaptive dynamics, evolutionary stable strategies (ESS), Lotka-Volterra models, coalescent theory, Price equation, diffusion models, evolutionary optimization, and computational simulations.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 9: Behavioral & Social Evolution
item.description: Evolution of behavior and social structures — altruism, kin selection, reciprocal altruism, evolutionary game theory, sexual selection strategies, mate choice, social insect evolution, eusociality, and cultural transmission of behavior.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 8: Evolutionary Developmental Biology (Evo-Devo)
item.description: Evo-Devo — how developmental processes drive evolutionary change. Hox genes, gene regulatory networks, modularity, morphological novelty, heterochrony, phenotypic plasticity, and constraints shaping body plan evolution.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 7: Mass Extinctions & Biodiversity
item.description: The Big Five mass extinction events, causes of extinction (climate change, asteroid impacts, volcanism), biodiversity patterns, speciation vs extinction rates, latitudinal diversity gradient, genetic diversity preservation, evolutionary rescue, and Anthropocene extinction risks.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 6: Co-evolution & Symbiosis
item.description: Species interactions and co-evolutionary dynamics — mutualism, commensalism, parasitism, predator-prey arms races, host-parasite evolution, pollinator-plant relationships, endosymbiosis, microbiome evolution, and the holobiont concept.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 5: Human Evolution & Migration
item.description: The hominin lineage from early primates through Homo sapiens — Australopithecines, Homo erectus, fossil & genetic evidence, ancient DNA, Neanderthal introgression, Denisovans, population bottlenecks, language emergence, and gene-culture coevolution.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 4: Phylogenetics & Taxonomy
item.description: Cladogram branch rotation, tree thinking, and phylogenetic relationships — why rotating branches around a node does not change relationships. Cladistics, monophyletic vs paraphyletic groups, molecular phylogenetics, Bayesian methods, and modern genomic classification.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 3: Speciation & Adaptive Radiation
item.description: Species concepts, modes of speciation (allopatric, peripatric, parapatric, sympatric), adaptive speciation, ecological speciation, magic traits, sensory drive, reinforcement, reproductive isolation, adaptive radiation, ecological niches, island evolution, and macroevolutionary patterns.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 2: Genetics of Evolution
item.description: Molecular foundations of evolution — DNA structure, mutation types, gene expression, recombination, population genetics, Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, genetic drift, gene flow, quantitative genetics, neutral theory, and molecular clocks.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Evolutionary Biology Series Part 1: Darwin, Wallace & Natural Selection
item.description: Explore the foundations of evolutionary theory — Darwin and Wallace
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 28: Emerging ARMv9 & Future Directions
item.description: ARMv9.0 (2021) was the first major architectural version since ARMv8 in 2011. It mandated SVE2 for server-class cores, introduced the Realm Management Extension for confidential computing, and continued a decade-long trend of adding new feature flags instead of breaking instruction semantics. This
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 20: Clinical Biochemistry & Diagnostics
item.description: Clinical biochemistry — blood glucose testing, HbA1c, liver function tests (ALT, AST, GGT, ALP, bilirubin), kidney function markers (creatinine, BUN, eGFR), lipid panels, cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP), and point-of-care testing.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 27: Security Research & Exploitation on ARM64
item.description: Security research on ARM64 blends intimate knowledge of the ISA with an understanding of OS memory layout, compiler mitigations, and hardware defences. This part covers the attack path from memory disclosure through ROP/JOP chain execution, examines kernel exploitation patterns specific to AArch64,
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 19: Molecular Basis of Disease
item.description: Molecular disease mechanisms — diabetes (type 1 & 2), cancer metabolism (Warburg effect), neurodegeneration (Alzheimer
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 25: Cross-Compilation & Build Systems
item.description: Cross-compiling for ARM64 on an x86 host seems straightforward until ABI mismatches, missing sysroots, and LLVM triple confusion bite you. This part covers correct toolchain selection for both bare-metal and Linux targets, CMake toolchain files, LLVM/Clang cross setup, and automated firmware
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 26: ARM in Real Systems — Android, RTOS & Bootloaders
item.description: Theory is validated in production. This part walks through five real deployment environments where ARM assembly knowledge is directly exercised: Android native code via the NDK, two embedded RTOSes (FreeRTOS and Zephyr), the U-Boot bootloader that brings up hundreds of millions of Linux boards, and
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 18: Tissue-Specific Metabolism Integration
item.description: Integrative metabolism — fed vs fasting state, organ fuel selection (brain, muscle, liver, adipose), starvation adaptation, Cori & alanine cycles, metabolic hormonal control, and exercise physiology.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 24: Linkers, Loaders & Binary Format Internals
item.description: Every ARM binary you run passed through a linker that stitched object files together, assigned addresses, and emitted relocations that the dynamic loader resolves at runtime. This part walks from raw ELF sections through RELA relocation entries, PLT/GOT lazy binding, position-independent code on
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 17: Adipose Tissue & Whole-Body Energy Balance
item.description: Adipose tissue biochemistry — triglyceride storage, lipolysis regulation, white vs brown adipose tissue, leptin & adiponectin signaling, adipose inflammation, body weight regulation, and obesity biochemistry.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 23: Debugging & Tooling Ecosystem
item.description: The ARM debugging ecosystem spans from QEMU software simulation up to hardware probes with instruction-level trace. This part covers the GDB remote serial protocol, OpenOCD configuration for JTAG and SWD adapters, ETM/ITM trace decoding, and practical kernel debugging workflows with QEMU. # ──
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 16: Immune System Biochemistry
item.description: Immune system biochemistry — antibody structure & classes, antigen-antibody interactions, cytokine signaling, complement cascade, oxidative burst in phagocytes, MHC & antigen presentation, and immunodeficiency disorders.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 22: Virtualization Extensions
item.description: ARM's Virtualization Extensions give EL2 a hardware-enforced isolation boundary between the hypervisor and every guest OS. This part walks the full path from EL3 dropping into EL2, configuring trap controls, programming two-stage page tables, wiring the virtual GIC, and understanding how KVM uses
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 15: Digestive System Biochemistry
item.description: Digestive biochemistry — gastric acid secretion, pancreatic enzymes, bile salt emulsification, carbohydrate/protein/lipid digestion and absorption, gut microbiome metabolism, and short-chain fatty acids.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 21: ARM Microarchitecture Deep Dive
item.description: Understanding what happens below the ISA boundary explains every counter-intuitive performance result from Part 18. This part maps the journey from instruction fetch and branch prediction down through register renaming, the reorder buffer, execution ports, store queues, and the cache/TLB hierarchy.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 14: Endocrine System Biochemistry
item.description: Hormone biochemistry — peptide vs steroid vs amine hormones, receptor mechanisms, insulin & glucagon signaling, thyroid hormones, cortisol & stress response, reproductive hormones, and endocrine disorders.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 20: Building a Bare-Metal OS Kernel
item.description: Building an OS kernel from scratch on ARM64 is the ultimate integration of everything in this series: boot sequence, exception vectors, memory management, and scheduling all collide in a few hundred lines of assembly and C. This part walks through each layer, from the first instruction after reset
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 13: Kidney Biochemistry & Acid–Base Control
item.description: Explore renal biochemistry — glomerular filtration, tubular reabsorption, ion transport, acid-base balance, bicarbonate reclamation, ammonia excretion, hormonal regulation (ADH, aldosterone, ANP), and kidney biomarkers (creatinine, BUN, eGFR).
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 19: Reverse Engineering & ARM Binary Analysis
item.description: Reverse engineering flips the assembly workflow: instead of writing instructions, you read them to understand what a binary does. ARM's ubiquity across Android, iOS, and embedded firmware makes it the most important architecture for binary analysis today. Mastering ELF layout, symbol conventions,
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 12: Liver Biochemistry
item.description: Explore liver biochemistry — glucose homeostasis, glycogen storage, gluconeogenesis, lipogenesis, beta-oxidation, urea cycle, bile acid synthesis, detoxification via cytochrome P450, and bilirubin metabolism.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 12: Regional Dissection Mastery
item.description: Comprehensive regional dissection mastery guide — upper limb, lower limb, thorax, abdomen, pelvis and perineum, head and neck, and back anatomy with clinical correlations, surgical approaches, and integrative dissection techniques.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: ARM Assembly Part 18: Performance Profiling & Micro-Optimization
item.description: Writing fast assembly is half craft, half measurement. ARM's Performance Monitoring Unit gives you hardware-level insight into every stall, cache miss, and mispredicted branch. Combined with Linux perf and a principled micro-benchmarking methodology, you can systematically identify and eliminate
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Biochemistry Series Part 11: Heart & Muscle Biochemistry
item.description: Explore heart and muscle biochemistry — cardiac metabolism (fatty acid preference, ischemia), skeletal muscle energy systems (ATP-PCr, glycolytic, oxidative), actin-myosin cross-bridge cycle, calcium signaling in contraction, and exercise biochemistry.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Human Anatomy Series Part 11: Functional & Applied Anatomy
item.description: Comprehensive guide to functional and applied anatomy — biomechanics of movement, joint stability and muscle mechanics, posture analysis, gait cycle, organ system integration, pain referral patterns, and clinical applications of anatomical knowledge.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 1: Why Neural Networks & Their History - Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: Neural networks are the engine behind the most transformative technology of the 21st century. They power voice assistants, translate languages in real-time, generate photorealistic images, write code, and diagnose diseases from medical scans. But why did we need them in the first place? Consider
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 2: Building Blocks — Neurons, Weights & Activations - Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: The artificial neuron is the fundamental unit of every neural network. Inspired by biological neurons, it takes multiple inputs, multiplies each by a weight, sums the results, adds a bias, and passes the result through an activation function to produce an output. Think of it as a tiny
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 3: How Neural Networks Learn | Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: Imagine tuning a radio with dozens of knobs. Each knob position affects the clarity of the signal. Your goal: find the exact combination that minimizes static and maximizes clarity. Neural network training works the same way — except instead of a few knobs, you have millions of “weights” to adjust,
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 4: Build Your First Neural Network from Scratch | Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: In Part 1 of this series, we proved that a single perceptron cannot solve the XOR (exclusive or) problem. A single neuron can only learn linearly separable patterns — and XOR is fundamentally not linearly separable. This limitation, famously highlighted by Minsky and Papert in 1969, nearly killed
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 5: Neural Network Architectures Overview | Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: Neural networks are not one-size-fits-all. Over the past four decades, researchers have designed specialized architectures to tackle fundamentally different types of data and problems. Each architecture introduces a specific inductive bias — a structural assumption about the data that allows the
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 6: CNNs Deep Dive - Convolutions from Scratch | Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: Fully connected networks treat every pixel as an independent feature. For a modest 28×28 grayscale image, that means 784 input neurons — and if we connect them to just 128 hidden neurons, we already have over 100,000 parameters in the first layer alone. Scale that to a 224×224 RGB image and the
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 7: RNNs & LSTMs Deep Dive | Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: Feedforward neural networks (FNNs) process each input independently — they have no concept of order or context. When you feed an FNN a word, it has no idea what came before it. But language, time series, audio, and countless other real-world signals are inherently sequential: the meaning of “bank”
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 8: Autoencoders & GANs Deep Dive | Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: Unlike supervised learning where we have labels guiding our models, autoencoders learn entirely without labels. The goal is to discover a compressed representation — an encoding — that captures the essential features of the data. This is the foundation of generative modeling: if we can learn a good
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 9: Transformers & Best Practices | Neural Networks Mastery
item.description: In 2017, the landmark paper “Attention Is All You Need” by Vaswani et al. fundamentally changed deep learning. The key insight was radical: instead of processing sequences one step at a time (like RNNs and LSTMs), look at ALL positions simultaneously. This single architectural choice unlocked
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Deep Dive: GANs — Generative Adversarial Networks | PyTorch Mastery
item.description: Imagine a counterfeiter who tries to produce fake currency that looks identical to real bills, and a detective whose job is to catch the fakes. As the counterfeiter gets better at forging, the detective must sharpen their skills to tell real from fake. Over time, this adversarial competition pushes
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Deep Dive: GPT-2 Mini — Building a Language Model | PyTorch Mastery
item.description: A language model is, at its core, a probability distribution over sequences of tokens. Given a sequence of preceding words (or tokens), a language model predicts what comes next. This is the fundamental task behind GPT-2, ChatGPT, and all modern large language models — they learn to predict the
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Deep Dive: ResNet — Residual Networks from Scratch | PyTorch Mastery
item.description: Before ResNet was introduced in 2015, a perplexing phenomenon haunted the deep learning community: deeper networks performed worse than shallower ones . This wasn't simply overfitting — even the training error was higher for deeper models. A 56-layer plain convolutional network consistently
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Deep Dive: U-Net — Image Segmentation from Scratch | PyTorch Mastery
item.description: Image segmentation is the task of assigning a class label to every single pixel in an image. Unlike classification (which outputs one label for the whole image) or object detection (which draws bounding boxes), segmentation produces a pixel-perfect map that outlines every object's exact boundary.
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Deep Dive: YOLO — Real-Time Object Detection | PyTorch Mastery
item.description: Before diving into YOLO, it's important to understand where object detection sits in the broader computer vision hierarchy. There are three progressively harder tasks that a neural network can perform on an image: Object detection is dramatically harder than classification because the network must
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)

item.title: Part 1: Tensors, Autograd & GPU Fundamentals — PyTorch Mastery
item.description: PyTorch is an open-source deep learning framework developed by Meta AI (formerly Facebook AI Research). Since its release in 2016, it has become the dominant framework in research and is rapidly gaining ground in production environments. If you're learning deep learning today, PyTorch is the best
item.author: wasil.zafar@gmail.com (Wasil Zafar)
